ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Chilli sauces and pastes) used as closest parent; no specific Laksa Chili entry exists in FoodEx2.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Laksa Chili is approximately one tablespoon (15 g), used as a condiment or dipping sauce alongside a bowl of laksa noodle soup.

nutrient_source.title: Chili Sauce / Sambal — Nutrient Composition; HPB Singapore Foodo Database and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Sauce, chili, ready-to-serve (FDC ID 173948) used as closest compositional analogue for chili-based condiment paste
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB Foodo entry for 'Laksa Chili' as a standalone condiment. Values adapted from HPB Foodo sambal/chili paste entries and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy chili sauce (FDC 173948). Laksa chili is a spiced chili paste with oil, shrimp paste, and aromatics; macros reflect a typical blended chili paste composition. Energy calculated from macros using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of chili-based condiments and sambal pastes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for chili sauce and spiced pastes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Laksa Chili. GI estimated at 25 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for chili sauce and sambal-type pastes, which are high in capsaicin, fat, and fibre with low available carbohydrate content. GL per 15 g serving = (25 × 14.5 × 0.15) / 100 ≈ 0.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (620 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Laksa Chili to no more than one teaspoon per serving, as even a 15 g portion contributes approximately 93 mg sodium, and cumulative use across a meal can significantly elevate daily sodium intake beyond the recommended 2000 mg limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI ~25) and small serving size limit glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Laksa Chili has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 25 and a glycemic load of only 0.5 per tablespoon serving, making it acceptable in small quantities for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though the fat and sodium content should be monitored in the context of the overall meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content with elevated sodium; capsaicin may have vasodilatory benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Laksa Chili contains moderate fat (12 g per 100 g, predominantly unsaturated) and high sodium (620 mg per 100 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should use it sparingly as a condiment and account for its sodium contribution within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Laksa Chili is a pungent, aromatic chili condiment paste integral to Singaporean laksa cuisine. It is prepared by blending or pounding dried and fresh red chilies with shallots, garlic, lemongrass, galangal, candlenuts, turmeric, and belacan (fermented shrimp paste), then frying the paste in oil until fragrant. The result is a deeply flavoured, moderately spicy, and slightly oily paste with a complex umami backbone from the shrimp paste and a bright heat from the chilies. Texturally it is thick and paste-like, ranging from coarsely ground to smooth depending on preparation. In Singapore, it is served as a side condiment alongside a bowl of laksa noodle soup — a rich coconut milk-based or sour tamarind-based broth with rice noodles, prawns, fish cake, and tofu puffs. Nutritionally, Laksa Chili is calorie-dense relative to its small serving size (approximately 180 kcal per 100 g), with meaningful fat content from cooking oil and a notable sodium load from belacan. Its glycemic index is low (estimated ~25), making it minimally impactful on blood glucose when used in typical condiment quantities. The capsaicin content from chilies has been associated with anti-inflammatory and vasodilatory effects in peer-reviewed literature. Clinically, the primary concern is sodium for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free but is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to the shrimp paste component.
common_user_queries: What is laksa chili made of?
How many calories in laksa chili?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for Laksa Chili as a standalone condiment product. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo sambal/chili paste entries and USDA SR Legacy chili sauce data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the compositional analogy is strong and the source databases are approved primary sources.
notes: Laksa Chili is a condiment paste, not a main dish; serving size is approximately 15 g (one tablespoon). Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo sambal and chili paste entries and USDA SR Legacy FDC 173948 (chili sauce). vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the source databases for this condiment category. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this condiment. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this condiment. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this condiment. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this condiment. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as this is a traditionally prepared plant-oil-based paste with no hydrogenated fats. GI estimated at 25 (low) from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for chili sauce and sambal-type pastes. Halal status assumes belacan is from halal-certified sources as is standard in Singapore hawker preparation. Price SGD 0.50 preserved from input (Qi Ji - Funan, field survey data).
